content.text: What Is AWS Marketplace?

AWS Marketplace is a digital catalog hosted by AWS where independent software vendors (ISVs) publish software products that AWS customers can discover, try, and purchase with a few clicks. As of 2024, it contains over 15,000 listings from thousands of sellers across categories including security, machine learning, data and analytics, infrastructure, DevOps tools, and business applications.

AWS Marketplace is not a separate service you configure — it is a procurement channel integrated into your existing AWS account. When you subscribe to a Marketplace product, charges appear directly on your AWS bill alongside your EC2, S3, and Lambda charges. You do not receive a separate invoice from the vendor, and you do not need to set up a new billing relationship. This billing integration is one of the primary reasons enterprise buyers choose Marketplace over direct vendor procurement.

For software vendors, Marketplace provides access to millions of AWS customers, handles billing and payment collection, and provides a distribution channel that eliminates the need to build their own e-commerce infrastructure. AWS takes a percentage of each transaction (the exact percentage varies by product type and sales model), and vendors receive monthly payouts from AWS.
How AWS Marketplace Works

attrs.alt: AWS Marketplace architecture: sellers publish products, marketplace brokers billing and access, buyers deploy into their own AWS accounts

content.text: The diagram above shows the three parties in every Marketplace transaction: the ISV seller, AWS Marketplace as the broker and billing integration layer, and your AWS account as the buyer. Products run entirely within your AWS account — the vendor never touches your environment.

A critical security property: when you deploy an AMI-based product from Marketplace, it runs as an EC2 instance in your VPC, under your IAM roles, visible in your CloudTrail logs. The vendor published the AMI (a machine image snapshot) to Marketplace, but they have no ongoing access to that running instance. You control it entirely. Similarly, for SaaS products, the vendor's service runs in their AWS account, and you access it via an API or web interface — your data flow and network connectivity are under your control.
Product Delivery Models
AMI-Based Products

The most traditional Marketplace format. The vendor publishes an Amazon Machine Image (EC2 machine image) pre-configured with their software. You subscribe and launch the AMI in your own EC2 environment. You choose the instance type, VPC, security groups, and IAM role — all the standard EC2 settings. Charges include both the AWS EC2 infrastructure cost and a software fee set by the vendor (per hour, per month, or annual). Examples: database appliances, network security appliances, specialized OS distributions.
SaaS Products

The fastest-growing Marketplace format. The vendor operates the software in their own AWS infrastructure. You subscribe, authenticate, and use the software via a web interface or API. All infrastructure is the vendor's responsibility. Charges are a fixed or usage-based fee set by the vendor, separate from your own AWS costs. Billing is still consolidated on your AWS bill. Examples: Datadog, Splunk, MongoDB Atlas, Twilio, Snowflake.
Container Products

The vendor publishes Docker container images to an ECR repository accessible through Marketplace. You pull the images and run them on your own ECS or EKS cluster. This model gives you the most control over the runtime environment while still using the Marketplace billing integration. Examples: Kubernetes operators, sidecar proxies, specialized middleware.
Data Products (AWS Data Exchange)

AWS Data Exchange is integrated with Marketplace for data subscriptions. Data providers publish datasets — financial data feeds, weather data, geospatial data, healthcare datasets, satellite imagery — that subscribers can access directly in S3 or via API. This model is unique to data: when you subscribe to a data product, the data is delivered to your S3 bucket and can be queried with Athena, loaded into Redshift, or processed with Lambda. Examples: Refinitiv financial data, NOAA weather data, DigitalGlobe satellite imagery.
Professional Services

Some listings are not software but services — consulting engagements, managed security services, or implementation packages sold through the Marketplace billing mechanism. These are priced by project, by hour, or by month, and the charges appear on your AWS bill. This simplifies procurement for enterprises that want all technology spending to flow through a single vendor (AWS).
Pricing Models
Pricing Model
Description
Best For
Free
No charge; the product is open-source or freemium
Evaluation, open-source tooling
Hourly
Charged per EC2 instance-hour the AMI runs
Variable, burst workloads
Monthly
Fixed monthly fee regardless of usage
Always-on SaaS tools
Annual
Annual subscription with upfront payment; typically discounted vs monthly
Committed production use
Usage-based
Charged per API call, per GB processed, per user, etc.
SaaS with variable load
BYOL
Bring Your Own License — you provide a license key from the vendor; Marketplace charges only EC2
Existing vendor relationships
Free Trial
14–90 days free then auto-converts to paid; cancel anytime before trial ends
Evaluation before committing

BYOL (Bring Your Own License) is particularly important for enterprises migrating existing software to AWS. Many organizations have multi-year enterprise license agreements with vendors like Oracle, IBM, or Citrix. BYOL Marketplace listings let them run that licensed software on AWS without paying a second software fee — they bring the existing license key, and Marketplace charges only the EC2 infrastructure cost.
Subscribing to a Product
Via the Console
Go to AWS Marketplace (aws.amazon.com/marketplace) or search from the AWS console
Find the product and click 'View purchase options'
Review pricing, EULA, and support details
Click 'Subscribe' (for SaaS products) or 'Accept terms and subscribe' (for AMI products)
For AMI products: click 'Continue to Configuration' to choose region and version, then 'Continue to Launch'
Charges begin immediately for paid products (or after the free trial period)
Via the AWS CLI

# Subscribe to a Marketplace product via CLI (requires knowing the product ID)
# First, find the AMI ID for a Marketplace product using the console or API

# List your active subscriptions
aws marketplace-catalog list-entities \
    --catalog AWSMarketplace \
    --entity-type Offer \
    --filter-list 'Name=EntityId,ValueList=your-offer-id'

# Launch an EC2 instance from a Marketplace AMI (same as normal AMI launch)
# The AMI ID is in the product listing; you must have an active subscription
aws ec2 run-instances \
    --image-id ami-0123456789abcdef0 \
    --instance-type t3.medium \
    --subnet-id subnet-12345678 \
    --security-group-ids sg-12345678 \
    --key-name your-keypair \
    --count 1
Via AWS CloudFormation or Terraform

Once you have subscribed to a Marketplace product, you can reference the AMI in CloudFormation templates or Terraform configurations. The subscription stays active independently of whether instances are running. You are only charged when instances are running (for hourly-priced products).
AWS Marketplace for Enterprise: Private Marketplace

Large enterprises face a governance challenge with Marketplace: any IAM user with appropriate EC2 and Marketplace permissions can subscribe to and launch any of the 15,000+ products. This creates risk — a developer could inadvertently subscribe to an expensive product, or launch an unapproved product that has not been security-reviewed.

Private Marketplace solves this by letting administrators curate an approved product catalog. Only products explicitly added to the Private Marketplace catalog can be subscribed to by users in the account (or across an AWS Organization). Attempts to subscribe to non-approved products are blocked.
Setting Up Private Marketplace
Vendor Perspective: Selling on Marketplace

Software vendors who want to list products on AWS Marketplace must apply through the AWS Marketplace Management Portal and agree to the AWS Marketplace seller terms. The process includes identity verification, banking setup for payouts, and software security review for certain product types (particularly AMI products, which go through an AWS security scan).

Listing on Marketplace is free — there is no listing fee. AWS takes a service fee (typically 2–15% depending on the product type and sales model) from each transaction, and the vendor receives monthly payouts. Sellers have access to usage and revenue dashboards, customer data (de-identified in some contexts), and the ability to offer private pricing to specific enterprise customers via Private Offers.
Private Offers

The standard Marketplace pricing shown in the catalog is the 'list price.' For large enterprise deals, vendors can create a Private Offer — a custom pricing agreement with a specific customer, negotiated outside the standard catalog pricing. Private Offers can include custom pricing tiers, custom contract lengths, custom payment schedules, and custom EULAs. From the buyer's perspective, a Private Offer appears in their Marketplace account and they accept it like any other subscription, with the custom terms applying. The billing still flows through AWS.
Governance and Cost Management
IAM Permissions for Marketplace
Marketplace access is controlled by IAM. The key permissions to understand:
Permission
Effect
aws-marketplace:Subscribe
Allow or deny subscribing to new Marketplace products
aws-marketplace:Unsubscribe
Allow or deny canceling subscriptions
aws-marketplace:ViewSubscriptions
View current subscriptions
aws-marketplace:DescribeAgreement
View agreement details (pricing, terms)

To restrict Marketplace purchasing to administrators only, attach a Deny policy for aws-marketplace:Subscribe to all developer roles, and allow it only on a specific 'Marketplace Admin' role. This prevents developers from accidentally subscribing to paid products.
Marketplace vs Direct Procurement
Dimension
AWS Marketplace
Direct from Vendor
Billing
Consolidated on AWS invoice; one payment
Separate vendor invoice; separate accounts payable relationship
Commitment
Flexible; monthly or annual; cancel anytime
Often annual contracts with negotiation required
Speed
Subscribe in minutes; immediate access
POC → legal → procurement → provisioning can take weeks
Security review
AWS scans AMIs; vendor identity verified
Customer performs own due diligence
Enterprise pricing
Private Offers for custom pricing
Direct negotiation with vendor account team
EDP credits
Marketplace spend counts toward AWS EDP commitment
Does not count toward AWS spending commitments
Support
Vendor support only; AWS provides infrastructure support
Vendor support only

Enterprise Discount Program (EDP) is a key reason large companies prefer Marketplace over direct vendor procurement. EDP is an AWS program where enterprises commit to spending a minimum dollar amount with AWS over 1–5 years in exchange for discounts. When you purchase Marketplace products, those charges count toward your EDP commitment. Direct vendor contracts do not. For an enterprise with a $10M/year EDP commitment, routing $2M of software purchases through Marketplace instead of direct vendor contracts accelerates reaching the EDP threshold and increases the discount tier.
Evaluating Marketplace Products Before Subscribing
Not every Marketplace listing has the same quality level. Before subscribing to a paid product, particularly for production use, evaluate these dimensions:
Seller Verification and Trust

AWS verifies the identity of all Marketplace sellers, but that verification does not imply product quality, security, or support quality. Look for sellers who are established companies with a track record, have the 'AWS Partner' or 'AWS ISV Partner' badge visible on their listing, and have review scores with a reasonable number of reviews. New listings from unknown sellers with no reviews deserve more scrutiny.
Security Due Diligence for AMI Products
For AMI-based products that will run in your network:
AWS scans Marketplace AMIs for known vulnerabilities before listing, but the scan is not exhaustive and does not cover application-layer vulnerabilities
Check whether the seller publishes a software bill of materials (SBOM) or CVE response policy
Verify that the AMI is updated regularly — a product last updated two years ago likely contains unpatched vulnerabilities
Review what ports and services the AMI exposes by default; check the product's recommended security group settings
Check whether the product supports customer-managed encryption keys (KMS) for data at rest
Look for SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, or other security certifications from the seller
Support Model and SLA

AWS Marketplace products are supported by the ISV, not by AWS Support. If you subscribe to a paid product and encounter an issue, you open a support ticket with the vendor directly. Before subscribing, verify:
What support tier is included in the subscription price (email only? phone? 24x7?)
What are the SLAs for response and resolution
Is there a community forum or documentation site
Are AWS-specific integration issues (IAM, VPC, EKS) supported by the vendor or does AWS Support handle them

Practical tip: Most Marketplace products offer a free trial period. Use the trial to evaluate not just the software but the support — open a test ticket, measure response time, check documentation quality. Your experience during the trial predicts your experience in production.
Managing Active Subscriptions
Viewing Subscriptions

# List all your Marketplace subscriptions (active and pending cancellation)
aws marketplace-entitlement get-entitlements \
    --product-code your-product-code \
    --region us-east-1

# View all subscribed products via the Marketplace console
# Navigate to: AWS Marketplace → Manage subscriptions

# Get subscription details via CLI using the catalog API
aws marketplace-catalog list-entities \
    --catalog AWSMarketplace \
    --entity-type Offer \
    --region us-east-1
Canceling a Subscription
Canceling a Marketplace subscription stops future billing but does not automatically terminate running instances or delete data. The sequence for clean cancellation of an AMI-based subscription:
Terminate all EC2 instances launched from the Marketplace AMI
Delete any EBS snapshots created from those instances (if no longer needed)
Navigate to Marketplace → Manage subscriptions → Cancel subscription
Confirm the cancellation; billing stops at end of the current billing period
For SaaS products: cancel via the vendor's interface first to prevent data loss, then cancel the Marketplace subscription

Important: Canceling a Marketplace subscription does not stop running instances. If you cancel your subscription but leave EC2 instances running that were launched from the Marketplace AMI, you will not be able to launch new instances from that AMI, but existing instances continue to run (and incur EC2 charges). Stop instances before canceling.
AWS Marketplace and AWS Organizations

For enterprises using AWS Organizations with multiple accounts, Marketplace subscriptions are per-account by default. A subscription in the management account does not grant access in member accounts. However, AWS supports Marketplace management at the organizational level through two mechanisms:
Linked Account Billing

All Marketplace charges in member accounts within an AWS Organization are consolidated on the management account's bill. You see Marketplace line items per-account on the consolidated invoice. This gives you visibility into what each account is spending on Marketplace products without requiring member accounts to set up their own billing.
License Manager Integration

AWS License Manager can track Marketplace software licenses across an Organization and enforce usage rules. If you purchase a Marketplace product with a floating license (e.g., 100 seats that can be assigned to any account in the Organization), License Manager tracks seat consumption and prevents over-provisioning. This is particularly useful for AMI-based products where you pay per instance-hour — License Manager can cap how many instances of a paid AMI run simultaneously across all accounts.

# View license entitlements granted to your account by Marketplace
aws license-manager list-received-licenses \
    --filters 'Name=IssuerName,Values=AWS/Marketplace'

# Check license usage across an Organization
aws license-manager list-resource-inventory \
    --filters 'Name=ResourceType,Values=EC2Instance'

In a multi-account setup, you can also use Service Control Policies (SCPs) in Organizations to restrict Marketplace subscriptions across all member accounts, requiring central approval before any account subscribes to a new product. This is a common control in regulated enterprises with formal software procurement processes.
Common Marketplace Use Cases
Security Tooling

Security tooling is the largest category on Marketplace and one of the most natural fits. Enterprise security tools — SIEM systems like Splunk and Sumo Logic, network firewalls like Palo Alto's VM-Series, vulnerability scanners like Tenable, and endpoint detection tools — are available as AMI-based or SaaS subscriptions. The billing integration is especially valuable for security tools that CISOs want running in every account: instead of managing 50 separate vendor billing relationships for a 50-account Organization, charges flow through a single AWS consolidated bill.
Database Appliances

Specialized database products — including commercial versions of MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle (BYOL), SQL Server (BYOL), MongoDB, Redis Enterprise, and others — are available as AMI-based Marketplace products with pre-configured instances. These provide a faster path to a production-ready database configuration than building from scratch on a bare EC2 instance, with the vendor's recommended tuning applied.
Development Tools and IDEs

A growing category covers development tooling delivered as SaaS through Marketplace: Snyk (dependency vulnerability scanning), JFrog Artifactory (artifact management), HashiCorp Vault and Consul (secrets management, service mesh), and GitHub Actions runners. Procuring these through Marketplace simplifies the subscription workflow for development platform teams who manage many tools across many teams.
Data Products for Analytics

Organizations building data pipelines often need third-party data: financial market data, weather data, demographic data, satellite imagery, traffic data. AWS Data Exchange (integrated with Marketplace) provides subscriptions to these datasets that deliver data directly to S3 or via API. The data is immediately queryable with Athena, loadable into Redshift, or processable with EMR — no manual download or custom ETL to ingest external data.
Marketplace Pricing in Practice: A Worked Example

To make Marketplace pricing concrete, consider a scenario: you subscribe to a commercial WAF (Web Application Firewall) product priced at $0.05 per hour per instance through Marketplace, and you run it on a t3.medium EC2 instance ($0.0416/hour in us-east-1).

Your total hourly cost per instance: $0.05 (software) + $0.0416 (EC2) = $0.0916/hour. Monthly cost (730 hours): approximately $66.90 per instance. If you run the WAF in two AZs (high availability), monthly cost is ~$133.80. This appears on your AWS bill as two line items: EC2 for the instance cost, and a Marketplace line item for the software cost.

If you instead subscribed to an annual plan at $25/month flat rate, the break-even is 500 hours per month per instance — almost exactly a full month of continuous operation. For always-on production workloads, annual pricing is usually cheaper than hourly; for development or test environments that run part-time, hourly pricing avoids paying for idle hours.
Summary

AWS Marketplace is a curated digital catalog of 15,000+ third-party software products, data products, and services that integrate directly into your AWS account. Products are deployed in your own VPC and environment, and charges appear on your AWS bill alongside your infrastructure costs — eliminating separate vendor billing relationships. Key product formats include AMI-based deployments, SaaS subscriptions, container images, and data products via AWS Data Exchange.

For enterprise environments, Private Marketplace enables governance by restricting purchases to an administrator-curated approved list. IAM permissions control who can subscribe. Marketplace spending counts toward AWS EDP commitments, making it financially preferable to direct vendor procurement for organizations with large AWS spending commitments. Whether you are evaluating security tools, sourcing production-ready OS images, or procuring specialized data feeds, Marketplace provides a procurement path that keeps all cloud spending consolidated in a single billing relationship.
